factual-accuracy.requirement: States to hold or reduce dose of metoprolol and all AV-nodal blockers immediately (unless a cardiologist or electrophysiologist explicitly recommends otherwise)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response only says to “consider pausing or reducing metoprolol pending specialist evaluation unless contraindicated,” which is conditional and limited to metoprolol. It does not explicitly instruct to hold or reduce metoprolol and all AV-nodal blockers immediately, nor include the exception “unless a cardiologist or electrophysiologist explicitly recommends otherwise.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends a specific replacement non-rate-slowing antihypertensive agent not affecting AV node (amlodipine, thiazide, or ARB) as an alternative to a AV-nodal blocker
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses reducing or pausing metoprolol but does not recommend a specific alternative non–rate-slowing antihypertensive (e.g., amlodipine, a thiazide, or an ARB) to replace the AV-nodal blocker.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Implements immediate driving restriction until symptoms are controlled, physician-approved, or diagnosis established
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses monitoring, safety netting, and when to call 911, but it does not advise an immediate driving restriction or explicitly state to avoid driving until symptoms are controlled, physician-approved, or a diagnosis is established.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides specific 911/emergency return precautions listing at least 2 of syncope, presyncope, HR <40, chest pain
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes emergency return precautions by instructing to call 911 for syncope and also mentions presyncope/near-syncope as a symptom trigger for urgent escalation. These satisfy the requirement to list at least two of the specified warning signs.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends maintaining a home pulse or symptom diary or BP/HR log, and to bring in these items or knowledge atthe  next medical visit
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “symptom diaries” only in passing in the conclusion and does not explicitly recommend that the patient maintain a home pulse/symptom diary or BP/HR log and bring it (or the recorded information) to the next medical visit. It also does not give instructions to present these logs at follow-up.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies MCOT or auto-triggered extended event monitor for 14-30 days
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends extended ambulatory monitoring with either a 14-day patch monitor or MCOT up to 30 days, and also discusses auto-triggered event/loop monitors up to 30 days.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States monitor placement within 24-72 hours
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends initiating a 14-day patch monitor or 30-day MCOT and says to start it “immediately,” but it never specifies monitor placement within a 24–72 hour window. The required explicit timeframe (24–72 hours) is not stated anywhere in the monitoring plan.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides at least one backup option if MCOT delayed or denied (short admission telemetry stay, patch monitor, or direct EP referral)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides backup monitoring options beyond MCOT, specifically recommending a 14-day patch monitor and discussing event (loop) monitors and implantable loop recorders as alternatives if MCOT is not feasible due to insurance or logistics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States real-time provider notification, alerts, or 24/7 monitoring capability
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes MCOT as providing real-time analysis with automatic event detection and provider alerts, and it also mentions remote monitoring and safety checks triggered by alerts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Orders transthoracic echocardiogram within 1-2 weeks
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends ambulatory ECG monitoring (14-day patch or 30-day MCOT) and escalation criteria, but it does not mention ordering a transthoracic echocardiogram or specify a 1–2 week timeframe for obtaining one.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides medical necessity language mentioning progressive conduction disease, recurrent symptoms, or PR prolongation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly uses medical-necessity language describing progressive conduction disease (PR interval increasing from 200 to 240 ms) and recurrent symptoms (multiple ED visits for lightheadedness/fatigue/presyncope), and it highlights PR prolongation as a key justification for monitoring and escalation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States real-time monitoring needed for safety due to living alone, driving, or intermittent events
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly links safety to real-time monitoring by recommending MCOT with real-time alerts and noting it is especially suitable when the patient lives alone and drives, since alerts can trigger emergency response.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions peer-to-peer review, out-of-network request, or admission if authorization delays persist
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends escalation pathways if urgent pacing criteria are met, including “arrange immediate inpatient evaluation and pacemaker discussion” and notes “Absolute indications for inpatient admission and rapid pacemaker workup.” This satisfies the requirement to mention admission if authorization delays persist or safety thresholds are reached.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States specific pause duration requiring urgent EP (pause ≥3 seconds symptomatic or ≥5 seconds asymptomatic)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “Pauses >3 seconds” and later “pauses ≥3s,” but it does not specify the full urgent EP thresholds of ≥3 seconds when symptomatic or ≥5 seconds when asymptomatic. The ≥5-second asymptomatic pause criterion is missing, so the required specific pause-duration rule is not fully stated.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States HR <40 bpm with symptoms requires emergent evaluation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists urgent escalation triggers like Mobitz II/high-grade block, pauses >3 seconds, and symptomatic bradycardia, but it never states that a heart rate <40 bpm with symptoms specifically requires emergent evaluation. The explicit HR <40 bpm threshold is missing from the immediate pacemaker consultation criteria.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Lists at least 2 specific block types requiring same-day or urgent EP (Mobitz II, 2:1 block, high-grade AV block, or complete heart block)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists multiple urgent block types, including Mobitz II and high-grade (≥2:1) AV block/third-degree (complete) AV block, as triggers for immediate pacemaker evaluation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States syncope or true loss of consciousness requires emergent same-day evaluation (go to emergency room or call 911)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response advises safety netting with “when to call 911 (worsening symptoms, syncope)” and notes syncope as a symptom trigger for urgent escalation, implying emergent evaluation rather than outpatient workup.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies EP or cardiology consultation within 72 hours or states urgent timeline
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends initiating monitoring immediately and calls for “immediate inpatient evaluation and pacemaker discussion” if urgent criteria are met, but it does not specify EP/cardiology consultation within 72 hours or give an explicit urgent (e.g., 24–72 hour) outpatient consult timeline.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Continues metoprolol or recommends dose reduction by the patient without stopping
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response suggests "pausing or reducing metoprolol" rather than continuing it or recommending patient-led dose reduction without stopping. It does not advise the patient to keep taking metoprolol unchanged, nor does it recommend a dose reduction by the patient while continuing therapy.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Allows continued driving without restriction despite progressive conduction disease and recurrent presyncope
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not allow continued unrestricted driving; it emphasizes safety netting, urgent escalation for high-grade block/syncope, and notes increased risk due to living alone. It does not recommend that the patient keep driving as usual despite progressive conduction disease and recurrent presyncope.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Dismisses progressive AV block as benign, stable, age-related, or not concerning
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response treats progressive conduction disease as concerning, recommends extended monitoring, and lists multiple thresholds for urgent pacemaker consultation (e.g., Mobitz II, high-grade block, pauses, syncope). It does not characterize progressive AV block as benign, stable, age-related, or not concerning.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Delays monitoring to 4 or more weeks without urgent interim plan or advising to follow-up in a clinic
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends initiating a 14-day patch monitor or 30-day MCOT immediately and includes safety netting (911 thresholds) plus escalation to inpatient/pacemaker evaluation for high-risk findings. It does not suggest waiting 4+ weeks without an interim plan or clinic follow-up.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States for intermittent symptoms occurring over weeks, of all non-invasive remote monitoring options mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry (up to 30 days) offers the highest diagnostic yield with real-time arrhythmia detection and immediate clinician notification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry (MCOT) can be used up to 30 days and provides real-time detection with provider alerts/notifications, and it presents MCOT as the preferred/highest-yield option for capturing infrequent high-grade AV block in this scenario.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that the presence of left bundle branch block on electrocardiogram markedly increases the likelihood of underlying structural heart disease and of diagnosing left ventricular systolic dysfunction, which warrants urgent echocardiography in this setting of lightheadedness and fatigue
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses progressive AV block and monitoring/pacemaker thresholds but never mentions left bundle branch block, its association with structural heart disease or LV systolic dysfunction, or the need for urgent echocardiography in this setting.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies progressive AV block, high-grade block, or degenerative conduction disease as primary concern
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly frames the main issue as progressive conduction disease with concern for intermittent high-grade AV block, citing progressive PR prolongation with dropped beats and listing Mobitz II/high-grade block as urgent concerns.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recognizes metoprolol as a contributing factor
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes metoprolol as an AV nodal–blocking medication that can worsen bradycardia/conduction disease, including stating “New or worsening symptoms while on AV nodal blocking medications (as in this patient with metoprolol)” and recommending considering pausing or reducing metoprolol.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States that to maximize authorization success, documentation should emphasize all of the following: (1) recurrent ED visits with symptoms potentially attributable to bradycardia, (2) progressive ECG changes (PR prolongation, dropped beats), (3) inability to capture arrhythmia during brief ED monitoring, and (4) high-risk features (elderly, lives alone, drives daily).
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response advises emphasizing recurrent ED visits, progressive ECG changes (PR prolongation and dropped beats), inability to capture arrhythmia during brief ED telemetry, and high-risk features including living alone and older age (and notes driving daily in the case context). These elements are presented in the documentation/authorization section as key to medical necessity and appeals.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes response into distinct sections for assessment, immediate management, monitoring strategy, and escalation thresholds
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with distinct sections covering assessment (clinical context/problem overview), immediate management (practical implementation/medication review and consensus pathway steps), monitoring strategy (ambulatory modality selection and duration), and escalation thresholds (guideline-based triggers for urgent pacemaker evaluation).

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses clinical terminology such as drug names, PR intervals in ms, HR thresholds in bpm, and block type classifications
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses clinical terminology including drug names (metoprolol), PR interval values in ms (PR 240 ms, PR 200 ms), heart rate values in bpm (HR 52), and AV block classifications (first-degree AV block, Mobitz II, high-grade/third-degree AV block).

presentation-quality.requirement: Places immediate safety actions (medication holds, driving restrictions, emergency precautions) in opening or clearly labeled immediate section
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes an early “Practical Home-Based Implementation and Risk Mitigation” section that advises safety netting (when to call 911) and medication action (consider pausing/reducing metoprolol), and later specifies thresholds for immediate inpatient evaluation/pacemaker discussion. These are clearly labeled safety actions rather than buried in the text.

citation-quality.requirement: References the "2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients with Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay" as definitive authority: https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000628
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites and links to the “2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients with Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay” (listed as source [12]) and refers to using ACC/AHA/HRS 2018 guideline recommendations as authoritative support.
